5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Falling-sand simulations are easy to watch and difficult to debug. A player sees a wall melt or water flash into steam. The program sees hundreds of local decisions performed in an order that may change the next decision. A bug that looks like “magma behaves strangely” may actually come from movement, heat transfer, update order, or a reaction that runs twice.
The fastest debugging method is not to stare at a large scene. It is to reduce the scene until every cell has a job and every transition can be explained.
Start with one claim, not one material
“Fire is broken” is too broad to test. Replace it with a claim that can be true or false:

• Fire should heat the adjacent ice cell after one update.

• Water should not pass through an inert wall.

• A powered wire should activate one lamp but not an unconnected lamp.

• A plant should burn only after its ignition threshold is reached.
A useful test scene is often only five to twenty cells. Draw an inert boundary, place one source material, one target material, and enough empty space for the expected output. Pause the simulation and advance it one step at a time if the engine supports that.
If the small scene works, add one variable. If it fails, you now have a compact reproduction.
Separate movement from reaction
Many particle engines mix two kinds of behavior in one update loop:

• movement, such as falling, flowing, rising, or swapping by density;

• reaction, such as burning, melting, cooling, dissolving, or conducting power.
When both happen in the same frame, a material may move away before its neighbor checks for a reaction. The opposite can also happen: a reaction creates a new material, and the new cell is updated again immediately because the scan has not passed its destination yet.
Add temporary trace records for each phase. For a watched cell, record its position, old and new material, temperature, selected neighbor, and transition reason. A trace such as water -> steam: temperature 102 is easier to inspect than a visual burst.
For larger engines, consider distinct phases: collect reactions, resolve conflicts, apply state changes, and move particles. Even if production remains in-place, this separation can reveal which phase owns the bug.
Make update order visible
In-place cellular automata are sensitive to scan direction. A cell that moves into a position that has not been visited may update twice. A cell that moves into an already visited position may wait until the next frame. Always scanning left to right can also make piles lean or liquids prefer one side.
Use a tiny asymmetric test to expose the order. Place one falling particle above a diagonal opening, run exactly one frame, and record its destination. Repeat with the horizontal scan direction reversed. If the outcome changes, update order is part of the behavior.
Randomizing scan direction reduces visible bias but makes bugs harder to reproduce. During tests, inject a seeded random function. A failure replayed with the same seed is more useful than “it happens sometimes.”
Treat temperature as data with units and limits
Heat bugs often come from hidden assumptions. One rule may treat temperature as degrees Celsius, another as an arbitrary energy score, and a third may clamp values without reporting it.
Write down the model explicitly:

• What is the default temperature?

• How much heat moves between neighboring cells per step?

• Are thresholds inclusive or exclusive?

• Does a phase change preserve, consume, or reset heat?

• Are values clamped, and at what limits?
Then test the threshold boundaries. If ice melts at 0, try -1, 0, and 1. If a plant ignites at 100, test 99, 100, and 101. Boundary tests catch more mistakes than repeatedly adding a stronger heat source.
Store the reason for every state change
A material ID alone tells you what a cell is now, not why it changed. During development, attach a lightweight reason code to transitions:
text
MOVED_DOWN
SWAPPED_BY_DENSITY
HEATED_BY_NEIGHBOR
MELTED_AT_THRESHOLD
IGNITED_BY_CONTACT
REACTION_PRODUCT
A debug build can keep this metadata only for the last frame or watched cells. When an unexpected product appears, the reason code narrows the search to one rule family.
Resolve competing reactions deliberately
Suppose a water cell touches magma, salt, and an electric conductor in the same frame. Which reaction wins? If the answer depends on array order, the simulation may behave differently after an unrelated material is added to the registry.
Give competing reactions an explicit policy: priorities, the most specific rule first, one reaction per cell per step, or a conflict-resolution pass. The important part is that the outcome is documented and testable.
Test two reactions competing for one cell. Assert the winner and the fate of the losing reaction so later content updates cannot silently change old scenes.
Measure performance without changing the experiment
Performance problems can masquerade as physics bugs. When the frame rate collapses, a time-based effect may appear slower, input may arrive in bursts, and a reaction may cover more cells before the next render.
Measure simulation time and rendering time separately. Run the same fixed scene at different canvas sizes and material counts. If an empty large world is slow, full-grid scanning is probably the issue. If a static full world is slow, rendering is a better suspect. Active chunks help the first case; a pixel buffer or GPU renderer may help the second.
Keep the reproduction scene and expected outputs constant while replacing one implementation detail.
Turn every fixed bug into a fixture
Save the smallest failing scene as a grid, JSON document, or compact text fixture. A useful fixture contains:

• the initial cells and temperatures;

• the random seed or fixed random choices;

• the number of update steps;

• the expected final cells;

• any expected transition reasons.
Run these fixtures after adding a new material or changing the update loop. Visual simulators still benefit from ordinary deterministic tests. The image on screen is the result; the rules underneath can be checked cell by cell.
A compact debugging checklist
Before expanding a broken scene, ask:

• Can I state the failure as one testable claim?

• Can I reproduce it with fewer than twenty active cells?

• Did movement or reaction run first?

• Could a cell have updated twice?

• Is randomness seeded for this run?

• Are threshold units and boundary conditions explicit?

• If reactions compete, is the winner deterministic?

• Is the slowdown in simulation or rendering?

• Did I save the fixed case as a regression fixture?
Small particle worlds become understandable when every change has an owner and a reason. Once a five-cell reproduction behaves correctly, scale it gradually. The large scene should be the final demonstration, not the place where debugging begins.
For a browser-playable reference with powders, liquids, gases, heat, electricity, and compact reaction examples, I keep an independent material-reaction guide at https://sandboxels.cc/guides/material-reactions/
It is not affiliated with the original Sandboxels project, and the debugging workflow applies to other grid-based particle simulations as well.
